speaker: interviewee
text: My sons will be here pretty soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're interviewing Mira Shelub, and this is tape number four. Mira, when we last left off on the prior tape, you were just telling us about your husband. And he had just escaped from Nowo--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowogrodek.

speaker: interviewer
text: --grodek. And has made his way now to the forest, to the partisans. And you had escaped from Dworez. And you were now also in the forest. And how did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the forest I joined the partisan group. And we were all suffering from a skin disease, which is-- they call it in Polish [POLISH], in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. I don't know exactly in English, but it itches terribly. And we were applying some kind of medication, you know, to all the parts where we were hurting. And I had it, and my husband-- my future husband had it. So we started to talk. And I told him that I studied in Nowogrodek. And he says, I come from Nowogrodek. And then I say I was with somebody by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. He says, this was my brother. So that's how we got acquainted. We started to talk to each other. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess we liked each other right away. But he was the leader, so he was going on these missions, and coming back. And then we started, in a way, to trust each other. And we promised each other that we will be devoted to each other forever, and ever, and ever. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] since then, we were together. I liked him very much. He was kind. He was nice. He was very handsome. He was a good husband. While we were in the forest, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we made contact with the Russian headquarters. And they supplied us with ammunition and with information about the front. Now, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Poland has a continental climate. Summer time gets very hot, and winter time gets very cold. And when it's cold, it snows. And when you walk on snow, it leaves marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to walk in different directions to confuse the enemy, so the enemy would not know where we're situated. At times we knew that the Germans are near us. And if we found out that it was a large group, that we will not be able to fight against. So we would move from one place to another. If we knew that it's not such a large group, that we'll be able to fight against them, so then we would put a resistance, put up a resistance. And we would fight against them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those weren't exactly Germans. The Polish police from the places around us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you put up resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would fight. We would fight against them. Just we knew that there are not too many. So we would fight against them. We had like, for instance, my husband carried a machine gun. And I carried-- he carried a machine gun, and I carried the bullet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the arms come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said before, we would get some arms from the peasants, and some we would get from-- we made contact with the Russian headquarters in the forest. So some we would get from them. Every time when my husband would leave on a mission, I never knew if he'll return. And I remember he would go on a white horse with a white coat. And I would say, oh, my gosh, I hope he'll come back. I hope he'll come back. And he did come back. We did survive the war. And he always was dreaming about [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means icicle. A little boy-- that his name will be [NON-ENGLISH] after his father. And I always used to say, we'll survive. We'll survive. I know that we'll survive. But we said that if something happens to us, so we should be together. We should go together. That's that. But we-- in 1944, we were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you talk about liberation I want to know a little bit more about--

speaker: interviewee
text: The forest?

speaker: interviewer
text: --the forest. You described more than one group to me, a family group, an armed group. Tell me-- describe a little bit more about the groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest we had two different groups, one was an armed group, young men and women belonged to this group.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 20, 25, close to 30, so on. In the family groups, we had elderly people. Not elderly, like for instance now, it's not like, for instance, a 50, 60 year olds, and young, young, young children. And where would they get the food? We would supply them with some food. And some would go at night to the closed villages and get the food from the peasants. For instance, my father, he used to live in a little village near shtetl. So during the war he would go at night-- just at night you can walk like that. He would walk to his little village and see the people who he knew from before the war. And they would give him some food, some clothing, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were peasants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peasants, friendly peasants, friendly peasants. The non-friendly peasants, they would report us to Germans. But the friendly peasants, they supplied us with food, with ammunition. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The role of the women in the forest was also a poor role. It was something, you know-- the women could not exist without the men. So some women would get together with men, and they had nothing in common, very different backgrounds. And after the war, they would separate. I was lucky enough that I met my husband and we had a lot in common. Both students with very similar backgrounds and we liked each other very much. And we stayed with each other till death separated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the women got together with men in the forest, are you talking about a personal relationship, or their relationship as a partisan?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, personal relationships. Personal relationships. They would get together. They would leave with each other. But after the war, they separated, because they were two different backgrounds. Two different people who got together, it's hard to put up. So they would separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why could a woman not exist alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she needed a man who would supply her with different necessary things. He would take care of her. He would worry about her. She would have a friend who cares for her and inspire her and support her. So that was something that she needed. And she couldn't exist-- there were very few women who could exist independently who were alone. But the majority had-- they called it tavos, tavo-- it means belongs to somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there in each of these groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like for instance, in our group that my husband was leading, we were about 140, 120 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: That included the family group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, just the armed group. The armed group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family group?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family groups, they were-- it depends. See, some of the family groups, they lived separately. They lived in a little cave under the ground, you know, where they were safe, supposedly secure. Some family groups, the men and the women, they live together, together in one hiding place. So it was different, not the same. Not the same. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: In yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In your group. OK. Like for instance, in my armed group, but I would like to give you another example. My mother and father, for instance, and my brother, they lived in a family group. And there were other people who lived around them. Then one day, not Germans, but the local police attacked them. And I was with my sister at a different place at that point. So we were approaching this place where my parents are. And we could see that something happened there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could see bodies all over, lying around. So we came to the place and we realized that my mother was gone. And we didn't know where she was. My father, they were in this little cave, this little hiding place. And then when they heard the Germans approaching them, they came out of this cave-- and my father didn't. He just was hiding behind this little cave. But my mother came out and she run straight in the Germans' hands. And she was killed. And my brother ran out. He was running naked, completely naked, didn't have time to dress. And then we met. And the people around us, they knew that mother was dead. So they brought us to the place where my mother was lying. And she died. She was just killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? She was shot. She ran straight into their hands not knowing where she was running.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that she was, at that point, she was in the 50s. 50s, can you imagine?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you when you found her?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just terrible. It was just horrible. We felt so bad about it. But it was nothing. What could you do? What could you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me what happened when you found her. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what I was going to do. I was just completely torn. I lost my mother, my mother who supported us, who was my help, my hope. And here she's gone. It was a terrible feeling. And my father, he never recuperated. Never. He just couldn't live without mom. And we were left, we were left alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What became of your mother? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We buried her. We buried her.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest. And you know, what hurts the most is that you don't even know where. You don't even know-- somewhere in the forest. I would never be able to find the place. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] You know, the only thing that made us feel a little bit more at peace is it was like I mentioned before, something normal, that we were not the only ones who lost loved ones, that we did not know what will happen to us tomorrow. And that was what kept us going, not knowing what will happen tomorrow. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And it was sad. Life completely changed at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, father became so-- he became so weak. He became so fragile, you know? He couldn't go-- I don't know. It's when I lost my husband, I felt it's over. That it was no use to go on. That I cannot possibly survive without him. And I think that father felt the same. He fell that it's no use to go on. He lost his life companion, so what's the use to go on? So it's just-- it's a terrible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any particular mission you went on that you want to describe for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mission. I was going not all the time with my husband. When my husband would go on a mission, we girls, we stayed on the campus. And we took care of things. We did the laundry. We took care of the campus. I mean, we did something. But at times I would go with him. I would go with him. But most of the time we were on the campus. And the men, they would go on the missions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a mission consisted of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mission consisted to blow up a train, to attack a local police station. And usually when they would go on a mission-- and not too many, let's say about 20 people, you know about 18 people. So the peasants would say, you know how many partisans were here? In the hundreds. Because we would come and make a big noise, psychologically, you know, that they'll think that a lot of partisans were here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'd say, you know how many? In the hundreds. And the Germans would get scared. Besides, it's scary to get into the forest, because you have a feeling that each tree is shooting at you. So the Germans would stay away from the forest. But the local police, they would attack us. And in 1944, we were liberated. We didn't believe that we are liberated. We didn't believe that we are again free men and women.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then our desire was to go back to our towns, to return. But we did not expect to find much, and we found much less than we expected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. And when we came to our towns, our loved ones were gone, our homes were destroyed. Shtetl did not look anymore like it used to. It was entirely something different. We couldn't even stay there. So we were allowed, as the Polish citizens, to leave our towns and to go deeper into Poland, into the center of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe for me when you found out that you were liberated. Tell me how you found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. When we were in the forest, at that point I wasn't even together with my husband. He was on a mission. He was coming, returning back to the base. And I was there with the girls. And we found-- at first we thought, these are not the Russians. They are Germans. It's not true. It's not that we are liberated, the Germans are in the forest. But then we realized they started to speak to us Russian. We realized that those were Russians. And then when we met with my husband, he was liberated at one place, and I was liberated another place. And we met. And it felt so good. I mean, I just didn't believe it that we are again free, that we can do whatever we desire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your liberation day to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nothing special. It was just a few Russians came to the forest, and they announced to us that we are liberated, that the Germans are no more here. The Germans escaped. That's right. We would see from here and there, single Germans wandering around. And they were not strong anymore. And I remember that they were asking, saying that I have a wife and I have children, please spare me. And some of the partisans, they were so angry, and they said-- one, especially one partisan-- he said this is for my mother, this is for my father, for my sister, for my brother, and killed him. He killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he kill him? Stabbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that partisan?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't do it. I just couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that partisan?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Israel now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is [NON-ENGLISH. No, Marily something. Marily. I couldn't-- I don't-- my husband could do it. You have to have a special nerve, I guess, to do it. But he said-- I cannot blame him. He lost his whole family. And the German was begging, I have a wife and children, spare me. He says-- killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were wandering around in torn clothes. It was cold. So they put whatever they found, they put around them. And it was just-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but we felt good. We felt good. We were liberated. We can go back to our towns. We can start a new life. Before you go to your new life, you were on some missions, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you on any missions?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I said, that we were mainly in the base. We were taking care of things. But I-- a couple times I went with my husband. I went with my husband. And I remember once we tried to blow up a train. The train did not come. And we had everything ready, and nothing happened, nothing happened. But my husband went a couple times with some partisans to the ghetto to save some Jews from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowogrodek. But it was too late. Like for instance, he returned to ghetto, he went back, as I mentioned before, to save his mother and sister-- his mother and brother. And they were gone already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did I mention that to you? I didn't. My husband went back to the ghetto to save his mother and brother. And that was too late because they were already put in a-- they were put in a truck and were driven away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband's brother, he was such a tall, blonde, handsome man. And the German begged him to escape. He was a good German. He wanted him to escape. And he says, I will not escape without my mother, unless you save my mother. They did save his mother. He left together with his brother, with his brother, and they never returned. And my husband would walk around always and say, one day they'll knock in the door. Somebody will knock in the door, and that is going to be my brother. But it never happened. It never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was walking around with guilt feelings, feeling bad about what happened. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Gosh. OK. Then after the liberation, we came to-- we were allowed as Polish citizens to escape to-- to escape, that's right-- not escape but to move to Lodz. Lodz was an industrialized city in Poland. We came to Lodz. And we did not stay too long in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we left Lodz illegally, as Greek Jews. We left Lodz for Prague. But then on the trains we got caught by the Russians. And they talked to us in Russian. They said [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, these are our Jews. They understand everything. They just make believe that they don't understand. True. We understood everything, but we made believe that we don't understand. And they sent us back to Poland. We tried for the second time. And the second time we succeeded. We left the Czechoslovakian border. And we left the Polish border for Czechoslovakia, Prague. And then Prague for Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague, I would say that we were in Prague, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would say about three days, four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do during those days when you were in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague? In Prague we had our own place. We dreaming about reaching the American zone. We did not do too much in Prague. We were making connections so we can reach the American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said you had your own place, that meant what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means that we had rooms. So we had our room with my husband and other two people. And we were dreaming about reaching the American zone. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] OK. And we [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] came to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Austria we were taken by machines, driven to Austria, to Bad Gastein. And there we were placed in displaced persons camps. At that point, we were already-- things were a little bit better organized. The UNRRA took us from Prague to-- in Prague they had the American zone-- so from Prague, to Austria, to Bad Gastein. In Bad Gastein, we were placed in displaced person camps, in beautiful hotels.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said UNRRA took you. Who is UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: UNRRA. UNRRA is an organization. You spell it U-N-R-R-A. They helped the survivors leave for America, mainly for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: UNRRA was not a Jewish organization. UNRRA was not a Jewish organization, but it helped the Jews. It helped the Yugoslavians. It helped the people who survived the war. They placed them in separate camps. And it helped them to move to America, or to-- but some of them, of the survivors, they left for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like for instance, when we came to Austria, to Bad Gastein, our aim was to go to Israel. We were dreaming about moving to Israel. We counted how many borders were left to cross. But when we were in Bad Gastein, we made contact with our relatives in America. How did we make the contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American soldiers helped us. We gave our letters to them. They sent our letters to their relatives. And their relatives sent the letters to our relatives in America. Once our relatives in America found out that we are the only survivors with beautiful families, they started to bombard us with letters to come to America. We felt, OK, we'll come for a little bit, and then we'll go to Israel. But we came to America, we raised a beautiful family, built up a business, and we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now to change tapes.
